questions.question: What is the dominant cost in a large video streaming platform?
options: CPU for the web servers
Bandwidth (egress) — serving terabits/sec of video to viewers
The database license
RAM on the app servers
questions.explanation: Serving video to millions of viewers means tens of terabits per second of egress, which dwarfs storage and compute costs. This is why a CDN isn't an optimization but the load-bearing decision that makes the economics viable.

questions.question: Why can't you just store the raw uploaded file and stream it to everyone?
options: Raw files are illegal to store
Different devices/networks need different qualities (renditions + ABR), origin can't serve petabytes of egress (needs a CDN), and transcoding can't be done synchronously
Raw files are too small
You actually can with no changes
questions.explanation: A single fixed-quality file fails across varied devices and fluctuating networks, can't be served at scale from origin, and can't be transcoded inline. You need multiple renditions with adaptive bitrate, a CDN for delivery, and an async transcoding pipeline.

questions.question: What does adaptive bitrate streaming (ABR) do?
options: Compresses all videos to one small size
Gives the player multiple quality renditions split into chunks, and the player picks the quality per chunk based on current bandwidth
Stores the video in the database
Encrypts each chunk separately
questions.explanation: ABR provides a manifest listing the video at several qualities, each chunked. The player downloads chunk by chunk and switches quality (e.g. 1080p → 480p → 720p) as bandwidth changes — degrading quality instead of stopping, which is why modern streaming rarely buffers.

questions.question: Why is transcoding done asynchronously with a worker fleet rather than during upload?
options: To make uploads slower on purpose
Transcoding into many renditions is CPU-heavy and slow; doing it async via a queue + workers lets uploads return fast and absorbs spikes
Because it must run on the client
Because databases require it
questions.explanation: Producing every rendition and chunking a video is expensive and slow. Doing it inline would make uploads time out and tie up servers. An async queue (Phase 6) accepts the upload immediately, drops a job, and a scalable worker fleet processes it in the background; the video flips to 'ready' when done.

questions.question: Where should the video bytes and the video metadata each live?
options: Both in the relational database
Video bytes in object storage behind a CDN; small queryable metadata in a database
Both in the CDN cache only
Bytes in the database, metadata in object storage
questions.explanation: Large video blobs belong in object storage (S3) fronted by a CDN — cheap, durable, scalable, and offloaded to the edge. Only the small, queryable metadata (title, status, duration) goes in the database. Putting video in the DB would bloat it and wreck performance (Phase 2).
